title: DMZ (Demilitarized Zone)
description: A network segment that sits between the internet and internal network, hosting public-facing servers with limited internal access.
fullExplanation: A DMZ is a network zone that provides an additional layer of security between the internet and the internal corporate network. Public-facing servers (web, email, DNS) are placed in the DMZ — they can be reached from the internet but have restricted access to the internal network. If a DMZ server is compromised, the attacker cannot directly reach internal systems. Implemented using two firewalls (true DMZ) or a three-legged firewall with a dedicated DMZ interface.

architecture.description: External network — all traffic considered hostile
Public-facing servers — web, email, DNS, VPN gateway
Corporate LAN — workstations, internal servers, databases

trafficRules.rule: Allow only specific needed connections (e.g. DB queries)

keyFacts: Two-firewall DMZ: separate firewall between internet-DMZ and DMZ-internal — most secure
Three-legged firewall: single firewall with 3 interfaces (internet, DMZ, internal) — cheaper
Compromise of DMZ server should NOT give access to internal network
DMZ servers should not initiate connections to internal servers (except specific allowed ports)
Honeypot: place decoy systems in DMZ to detect and study attackers
Cloud DMZ: security groups and NACLs in AWS/Azure replicate DMZ concept
